facts.text: Jack the Ripper murdered at least five women in the Whitechapel district of London in 1888, and the killer's identity has never been confirmed.
Agatha Christie is the best-selling fiction writer of all time, with over two billion copies sold worldwide.
The FBI's Combined DNA Index System (CODIS) contains over 14 million offender profiles used to solve crimes across the United States.
The TV series CSI: Crime Scene Investigation premiered on CBS and became one of the most-watched shows in the world.
The Zodiac Killer terrorized Northern California in the late 1960s and early 1970s, sending taunting letters and cryptograms to newspapers.
Fingerprinting was first used for criminal identification in Argentina in 1892 by police official Juan Vucetich.
Sherlock Holmes first appeared in Arthur Conan Doyle's 'A Study in Scarlet,' published in 1887.
The podcast Serial, which investigated the 1999 murder of Hae Min Lee, was downloaded over 300 million times.
The Antwerp Diamond Heist of 2003 saw thieves steal diamonds worth over $100 million from the Antwerp Diamond Centre vault.
The FBI was founded in 1908 as the Bureau of Investigation and was renamed the Federal Bureau of Investigation in 1935.
Criminal profiling was pioneered by FBI agents John Douglas and Robert Ressler, who interviewed dozens of serial killers in prison.
The movie 'The Silence of the Lambs' became only the third film to win all five major Academy Awards: Best Picture, Director, Actor, Actress, and Screenplay.
Scotland Yard, the headquarters of London's Metropolitan Police, gets its name from a street called Great Scotland Yard where it was originally located.
Mata Hari, the famous exotic dancer, was executed by firing squad in France in 1917 for spying for Germany during World War I.
The O.J. Simpson murder trial in 1995 was watched by an estimated 150 million people when the verdict was read.
Forensic entomology uses the study of insects found on a body to help determine the time of death.
Agatha Christie's character Hercule Poirot appeared in 33 novels and over 50 short stories.
The Pinkerton National Detective Agency, founded in 1850, was at one point the largest private law enforcement organization in the world.
Blood spatter analysis can determine the direction, speed, and angle at which blood traveled, helping reconstruct crime scenes.
The TV show Criminal Minds ran for 15 seasons and focused on the FBI's Behavioral Analysis Unit.
Ted Bundy confessed to 30 murders before his execution in 1989, though the actual number of his victims may be higher.
The Isabella Stewart Gardner Museum heist in 1990 remains the largest unsolved art theft in history, with stolen works valued at over $500 million.
Raymond Chandler created the iconic private detective Philip Marlowe, who appeared in seven novels starting with 'The Big Sleep.'
The true crime podcast 'My Favorite Murder' popularized the term 'murderino' for true crime enthusiasts.
DNA evidence was first used to convict a criminal in 1987, when Colin Pitchfork was found guilty of two murders in England.
The Brink's-Mat robbery of 1983 at Heathrow Airport netted thieves 6,800 gold bars worth approximately £26 million.
J. Edgar Hoover served as the director of the FBI for 48 years, from 1924 until his death in 1972.
The TV series Law & Order ran for 20 seasons in its original run and spawned multiple spinoffs including SVU.
The Black Dahlia case refers to the unsolved 1947 murder of Elizabeth Short, whose body was found severed in half in Los Angeles.
Forensic odontology, the study of dental evidence, has been used to identify victims and bite mark evidence in criminal cases.
Arthur Conan Doyle based Sherlock Holmes partly on Dr. Joseph Bell, a surgeon known for his remarkable powers of observation.
The BTK Killer, Dennis Rader, evaded capture for over 30 years before being caught in 2005 through a floppy disk he sent to police.
The Hatton Garden Safe Deposit burglary in 2015 was carried out by a group of elderly thieves, the oldest being 76 years old.
The Crime Junkie podcast, hosted by Ashley Flowers and Brit Prawat, consistently ranks among the top podcasts in the United States.
The movie 'Se7en' features a serial killer who patterns his murders after the seven deadly sins.
The FBI's Ten Most Wanted Fugitives list was created in 1950 and has led to the capture of hundreds of fugitives.
Luminol is a chemical that forensic investigators spray at crime scenes to detect trace amounts of blood invisible to the naked eye.
Aldrich Ames, a CIA officer, spied for the Soviet Union for nine years before his arrest in 1994, compromising over 100 intelligence operations.
The Nuremberg Trials, held after World War II, established that following orders is not a defense for war crimes.
Agatha Christie disappeared for 11 days in 1926, sparking a massive manhunt before she was found at a hotel registered under a different name.
Forensic ballistics can match a bullet to the specific gun that fired it by analyzing the unique markings left on the projectile.
The TV series Dexter follows a forensic blood spatter analyst who moonlights as a serial killer targeting other killers.
The Casefile True Crime podcast, produced in Australia, is known for its thorough research and the anonymous host known only as 'Casey.'
The Lufthansa heist at JFK Airport in 1978 netted $5.8 million in cash and jewelry, making it one of the largest cash robberies in U.S. history at the time.
Miss Marple, Agatha Christie's elderly amateur detective, appeared in 12 novels and 20 short stories.
The movie 'Zodiac' directed by David Fincher is based on Robert Graysmith's investigation into the real Zodiac Killer.
Robert Hanssen, an FBI agent, spied for the Soviet Union and Russia for 22 years before his arrest in 2001.
Forensic anthropology involves examining skeletal remains to determine age, sex, ancestry, and cause of death.
The Charles Manson trial in 1970-1971 was one of the longest and most publicized criminal trials in American history.
The TV series Mindhunter, based on the book by John Douglas and Mark Olshaker, dramatizes the early days of FBI criminal profiling.
The Great Train Robbery of 1963 in England saw a gang steal £2.6 million from a Royal Mail train.
James Patterson is one of the best-selling authors of all time, known for the Alex Cross series of detective novels.
The concept of 'modus operandi' in criminology refers to the particular method a criminal uses to commit crimes.
Making a Murderer, the Netflix documentary series, brought worldwide attention to the case of Steven Avery.
The Lindbergh kidnapping of 1932 was called the 'Crime of the Century' and led to federal kidnapping laws in the United States.
Forensic toxicology analyzes body fluids and tissues to detect drugs, alcohol, and poisons that may have contributed to a death.
The movie 'Knives Out' pays homage to classic whodunit mysteries while subverting traditional genre conventions.
Allan Pinkerton, founder of the Pinkerton Agency, foiled an assassination plot against Abraham Lincoln in 1861.
The Green River Killer, Gary Ridgway, confessed to killing 71 women, making him one of the most prolific serial killers in U.S. history.
True Detective's first season, starring Matthew McConaughey and Woody Harrelson, was praised as one of the best TV seasons ever produced.
The CIA was established in 1947 under the National Security Act signed by President Harry Truman.
Forensic psychology studies criminal behavior and mental processes to help law enforcement understand and predict criminal actions.
The Unabomber, Ted Kaczynski, carried out a bombing campaign over 17 years before being identified through his published manifesto.
The Clue movie, based on the board game, was released with three different endings shown in different theaters.
MI6, the UK's Secret Intelligence Service, was founded in 1909 and remained officially unacknowledged by the British government until 1994.
The Casey Anthony trial in 2011 ended in a not guilty verdict that shocked millions of Americans who had followed the case.
Forensic accounting traces financial records to uncover fraud, embezzlement, and money laundering.
The TV show NCIS became one of the most-watched scripted series in the world, averaging over 19 million viewers per episode at its peak.
The Zodiac Killer's 340-character cipher was not solved until 2020, more than 50 years after it was sent.
The D.B. Cooper hijacking of 1971 remains the only unsolved case of aerial piracy in commercial aviation history.
Agatha Christie's 'And Then There Were None' is one of the best-selling mystery novels of all time with over 100 million copies sold.
The FBI Academy at Quantico, Virginia, trains new FBI agents and serves as a research center for behavioral science.
Dateline NBC, which premiered in 1992, became known for its investigative crime reporting and the 'To Catch a Predator' series.
The Boston Strangler case in the early 1960s terrorized the city, with 13 women murdered in their homes over 18 months.
Locard's Exchange Principle states that every contact between a criminal and a crime scene leaves a trace.
The movie 'Gone Girl' is based on Gillian Flynn's novel about a woman who stages her own disappearance to frame her husband for murder.
Kim Philby was a high-ranking member of British intelligence who was secretly a double agent for the Soviet Union for decades.
The Son of Sam, David Berkowitz, terrorized New York City in 1976-1977, claiming a neighbor's dog told him to kill.
Forensic DNA phenotyping can predict a suspect's physical appearance, including eye color, hair color, and ancestry, from DNA evidence.
The TV series The Wire is often cited as one of the greatest television dramas ever made for its realistic portrayal of crime in Baltimore.
John Douglas, former FBI profiler, wrote the book 'Mindhunter' that inspired the Netflix series of the same name.
The Jack the Ripper murders generated over 600 letters sent to police, though most are believed to be hoaxes.
The Stieg Larsson 'Millennium' trilogy, beginning with 'The Girl with the Dragon Tattoo,' became a global crime fiction phenomenon.
Cold case units use advances in forensic technology to reexamine unsolved crimes, sometimes decades after they occurred.
The movie 'Rear Window' by Alfred Hitchcock features a photographer who believes he has witnessed a murder from his apartment window.
The FBI Behavioral Analysis Unit was established in the 1970s to study serial offenders and develop profiling techniques.
The Moors Murders in 1960s England involved Ian Brady and Myra Hindley, who killed five children and buried them on Saddleworth Moor.
Forensic linguistics analyzes language patterns in threatening letters, ransom notes, and other documents to identify suspects.
Dateline NBC's Keith Morrison became a pop culture icon known for his distinctive narrative style on true crime segments.
The Scopes Trial of 1925, though not a criminal trial in the traditional sense, became one of the most famous courtroom dramas in American history.
The TV show Forensic Files ran for 14 seasons and used real forensic evidence to explain how crimes were solved.
The Night Stalker, Richard Ramirez, was captured in 1985 after residents in an East Los Angeles neighborhood recognized and subdued him.
Arthur Conan Doyle tried to kill off Sherlock Holmes in 'The Final Problem' but brought him back due to public demand.
Polygraph tests, commonly called lie detectors, measure physiological responses but are generally not admissible as evidence in court.
The movie 'Mystic River' directed by Clint Eastwood explores how a childhood crime affects three men decades later.
The French Sûreté Nationale, one of the world's first detective agencies, was founded by Eugène François Vidocq, a former criminal.
The Golden State Killer, Joseph DeAngelo, was identified in 2018 through genealogical DNA databases after evading capture for over 40 years.
The FBI maintains the Violent Criminal Apprehension Program (ViCAP), a database designed to collect and analyze information about violent crimes.
The TV show Broadchurch explored the impact of a child's murder on a small coastal town in England.
Serial killer Jeffrey Dahmer was arrested in 1991 after one of his intended victims escaped and led police to his apartment.
Forensic serology is the analysis of blood, saliva, semen, and other body fluids to link suspects to crime scenes.
The Sherlock Holmes stories introduced many concepts now standard in detective fiction, including the magnifying glass and forensic science.
The Enfield Poltergeist case of 1977 in London remains one of the most documented and debated paranormal investigation cases.
Crime Junkie's Ashley Flowers founded the media company audiochuck, which produces multiple true crime and mystery podcasts.
The trial of Lizzie Borden in 1893 for the axe murders of her father and stepmother ended in acquittal despite strong circumstantial evidence.
Cadaver dogs are specially trained to detect the scent of human decomposition and have been used in criminal investigations worldwide.
The movie 'Primal Fear' features a famous twist ending involving a defendant who may be faking multiple personality disorder.
The Cambridge Five were a ring of British spies who passed information to the Soviet Union from the 1930s through the 1950s.
H.H. Holmes is often called America's first serial killer, building a 'Murder Castle' in Chicago during the 1893 World's Fair.
The TV show Law & Order: SVU, starring Mariska Hargitay, became the longest-running prime-time live-action series in U.S. television history.
Forensic botany uses plant evidence, such as pollen and leaf fragments, to help establish timelines and locations in criminal investigations.
The Zodiac Killer sent a total of four cryptograms to newspapers, and two remain unsolved to this day.
Patricia Cornwell's Kay Scarpetta series features a forensic pathologist as the protagonist and has sold over 100 million copies.
The movie 'A Few Good Men' features one of cinema's most famous courtroom exchanges: 'You can't handle the truth!'
The Alcatraz escape of 1962, in which three inmates vanished, remains one of the most famous unsolved mysteries in American criminal history.
INTERPOL, the International Criminal Police Organization, facilitates cooperation among police forces from 194 member countries.
The true crime genre experienced a massive boom in the 2010s, driven by podcasts, Netflix documentaries, and social media.
Geographic profiling is a technique that uses the locations of connected crimes to estimate the likely area where a serial offender lives.
The TV series Columbo featured Peter Falk as a seemingly bumbling detective who always solved the case with his 'just one more thing' approach.
The Whitey Bulger case revealed that the FBI informant program had been corrupted, with agents protecting a violent crime boss.
Agatha Christie's play 'The Mousetrap' has been running continuously in London's West End since 1952, making it the longest-running show in history.
GIS mapping technology is now used by law enforcement to identify crime hotspots and allocate resources more effectively.
The movie 'Chinatown' starring Jack Nicholson is considered one of the greatest mystery films ever made.
The advent of DNA profiling in the 1980s by Sir Alec Jeffreys revolutionized forensic science and criminal investigation.
Ed Gein, a murderer and body snatcher in 1950s Wisconsin, inspired fictional killers including Norman Bates, Leatherface, and Buffalo Bill.
The Innocence Project has used DNA evidence to exonerate over 375 wrongfully convicted individuals in the United States.
The TV show Unsolved Mysteries, originally hosted by Robert Stack, brought public attention to cold cases and missing persons.
The Birmingham Six were wrongfully convicted of the 1974 IRA pub bombings and spent 16 years in prison before their convictions were overturned.
Forensic photography is used to document crime scenes, evidence, and injuries in a way that preserves their condition for court proceedings.
Philip Marlowe, Raymond Chandler's detective, embodied the hardboiled private eye archetype in American crime fiction.
The Tylenol murders of 1982 in Chicago led to the creation of tamper-resistant packaging for over-the-counter medications.
The movie 'The Usual Suspects' features one of cinema's greatest plot twists, revealing the identity of the mysterious Keyser Söze.
The TV show Twin Peaks, created by David Lynch, blended mystery with surrealism in its investigation of Laura Palmer's murder.
Robert Ressler, the FBI agent who coined the term 'serial killer,' conducted extensive interviews with imprisoned murderers to develop profiling methods.
The Dewey Decimal System classification 364 covers the subject of criminology in libraries worldwide.
The Central Park Five were wrongfully convicted teenagers whose case was overturned after a serial rapist confessed and DNA evidence confirmed his guilt.
Forensic digital analysis recovers deleted files, examines email trails, and traces online activity to solve cybercrimes.
The TV show Breaking Bad, while primarily about drug manufacturing, features detailed depictions of forensic investigation and law enforcement tactics.
The Manson Family murders, including the killing of actress Sharon Tate, shocked America in the summer of 1969.
Edgar Allan Poe is credited with creating the modern detective story with 'The Murders in the Rue Morgue' in 1841.
The Stockholm Bank robbery of 1973 gave rise to the term 'Stockholm Syndrome,' describing hostages who develop positive feelings toward captors.
The movie 'In Cold Blood,' based on Truman Capote's book, dramatized the real murder of the Clutter family in Kansas.
The FBI Laboratory, established in 1932, is one of the largest and most comprehensive forensic laboratories in the world.
The Yorkshire Ripper investigation in the late 1970s was one of the largest manhunts in British history before Peter Sutcliffe was caught.
True crime author Ann Rule worked alongside serial killer Ted Bundy at a crisis hotline before he was identified as a murderer.
Forensic palynology, the study of pollen grains found on evidence, can link suspects to specific geographic locations.
The TV show Luther stars Idris Elba as a brilliant but emotionally impulsive London detective.
The Brinks robbery of 1950 in Boston was called the 'crime of the century' and netted the thieves $2.7 million in cash, checks, and money orders.
Walter Dew was the detective who captured Dr. Crippen, one of the first criminals apprehended with the aid of wireless telegraphy.
The Bertillon system, developed in the 1880s, was the first scientific method of criminal identification based on body measurements.
The movie 'Memories of Murder' by Bong Joon-ho is based on South Korea's first serial murder case, which went unsolved for decades.
The TV show Killing Eve features a cat-and-mouse dynamic between an MI5 officer and a psychopathic assassin.
The JonBenét Ramsey case, which began in 1996, remains one of the most high-profile unsolved murders in the United States.
Hair and fiber analysis can place a suspect at a crime scene by matching microscopic characteristics of shed hair or clothing fibers.
Dashiell Hammett, a former Pinkerton detective, used his real-world experience to write crime fiction, including 'The Maltese Falcon.'
The Mafia Commission Trial of 1985-1986 resulted in convictions of the heads of New York's Five Families of organized crime.
Crime scene investigators wear protective clothing not only to protect themselves but to avoid contaminating evidence with their own DNA.
The movie 'Zodiac' was notable for using thousands of pages of police reports and evidence files to ensure accuracy.
The TV show Cold Case ran for seven seasons and focused on a Philadelphia detective reopening unsolved homicides.
The FBI's Behavioral Science Unit, formed in 1972, became the foundation for modern criminal profiling techniques.
Dr. Henry Lee is one of the most famous forensic scientists in the world, having worked on over 6,000 criminal cases.
The Great Diamond Heist of Antwerp in 2003 was so meticulously planned that it was dubbed the 'heist of the century.'
Rigor mortis, the stiffening of the body after death, is one of several indicators forensic pathologists use to estimate time of death.
The movie 'L.A. Confidential' is a neo-noir crime film set in 1950s Los Angeles, adapted from James Ellroy's novel.
The TV show The Jinx documented the life of real estate heir Robert Durst, who appeared to confess to murder on a hot microphone.
The West Memphis Three spent 18 years in prison before entering Alford pleas, maintaining their innocence of the 1993 murders of three boys.
Automated fingerprint identification systems (AFIS) can search millions of fingerprint records in seconds to find potential matches.
Sue Grafton wrote the 'Alphabet Series' of detective novels, starting with 'A Is for Alibi' and ending with 'Y Is for Yesterday.'
The Night of the Long Knives in 1934, when Hitler purged rivals, was investigated decades later as a crime against humanity.
The Rosenberg trial and execution in 1953 for passing nuclear secrets to the Soviet Union remains one of the most controversial espionage cases in U.S. history.
Toxicology screens in autopsies can detect over 200 different substances, from prescription drugs to exotic poisons.
The TV show Bones was inspired by the life and novels of forensic anthropologist Kathy Reichs.
Willie Sutton, the famous bank robber, reportedly said he robbed banks 'because that's where the money is,' though he later denied the quote.
Forensic facial reconstruction uses skull measurements to recreate the appearance of unidentified victims.
The movie 'No Country for Old Men' explores the aftermath of a drug deal gone wrong in the Texas desert.
The Catch Me If You Can story of Frank Abagnale Jr., who impersonated a pilot, doctor, and lawyer, became both a book and a Spielberg film.
Criminal Minds introduced millions of viewers to the concept of behavioral analysis and criminal profiling as used by the FBI.
The St. Valentine's Day Massacre of 1929 in Chicago, attributed to Al Capone's gang, killed seven members of a rival gang.
Trace evidence, such as paint chips, glass fragments, and soil samples, can provide crucial links between suspects and crime scenes.
G.K. Chesterton's Father Brown stories feature a Catholic priest who solves crimes by understanding the criminal mind.
The Green River Killer case was solved using DNA evidence after investigators matched semen samples to Gary Ridgway.
The movie 'Prisoners' explores the moral dilemmas faced when a father takes justice into his own hands.
The TV show Sherlock, starring Benedict Cumberbatch, updated the classic detective to modern-day London.
The Smiley Face Murder Theory proposes that a serial killer or killers are responsible for the drowning deaths of young men across the United States.
Allan Pinkerton's detective agency was hired by Abraham Lincoln as a personal security detail, predating the Secret Service.
Forensic geology uses soil and mineral analysis to connect suspects or evidence to specific locations.
The podcast 'Casefile True Crime' has covered hundreds of cases and is known for its meticulous and respectful approach to storytelling.
The movie 'Double Indemnity' is a classic film noir about an insurance salesman drawn into a murder plot.
The TV series Fargo, inspired by the Coen Brothers film, tells standalone crime stories set in the Midwest across multiple seasons.
Elizabeth Báthory, a Hungarian noblewoman in the 16th century, is believed to have tortured and killed hundreds of young women.
Latent fingerprints, invisible to the naked eye, can be developed using powders, chemicals, or alternative light sources.
Michael Connelly's Harry Bosch series follows an LAPD detective and has been adapted into a popular Amazon television series.
The Ripper Crew, also known as the Chicago Rippers, was a satanic cult that committed a series of murders in 1981-1982.
The FBI's Uniform Crime Reporting program has collected data on crimes reported to law enforcement since 1930.
The movie 'Vertigo' by Alfred Hitchcock is built around themes of obsession, deception, and murder.
The Silk Road, an online black market, was shut down by the FBI in 2013, and its founder Ross Ulbricht was sentenced to life in prison.
Crime scene tape, typically yellow, was first used in the 1960s to secure investigation areas and keep the public away.
The podcast 'Serial' launched the golden age of true crime podcasts when its first season debuted in 2014.
Forensic voice analysis compares voice recordings to identify or eliminate suspects based on speech patterns and vocal characteristics.
The TV show The Sinner explores the psychology behind why people commit violent crimes.
John Wayne Gacy, known as the 'Killer Clown,' murdered 33 young men and buried most of them under his house in suburban Chicago.
Ipek Williamson is known as a pioneer in the field of forensic art, helping identify victims through age-progression images.
The movie 'Fargo' by the Coen Brothers opens with a claim to be based on a true story, which is itself a fictional device.
Cesare Lombroso, a 19th-century criminologist, believed criminals could be identified by physical characteristics, a theory now discredited.
The Tate-LaBianca murders committed by the Manson Family effectively ended the optimistic spirit of the 1960s counterculture.
The TV show Psych features a hyper-observant consultant who pretends to be psychic to help the Santa Barbara police solve crimes.
The Gardner Museum in Boston still displays empty frames where the stolen paintings once hung, hoping for their return.
Forensic document examination analyzes handwriting, typewriting, and printing to detect forgeries and authenticate documents.
Thomas Harris created the fictional serial killer Hannibal Lecter, who first appeared in the novel 'Red Dragon' in 1981.
The Watergate scandal led to President Nixon's resignation, proving that criminal investigations can reach the highest levels of government.
The Night Stalker Richard Ramirez used no consistent method, making him difficult to profile and track.
The movie 'The Thin Man' series popularized the witty detective couple Nick and Nora Charles in 1930s Hollywood.
The TV show Marcella explores a former detective who returns to work on a case similar to an old unsolved serial murder.
Emile Zola's open letter 'J'accuse' in the Dreyfus Affair is one of history's most famous interventions in a criminal case.
Forensic entomologists can determine the postmortem interval by studying the life cycles of blow flies and beetles found on remains.
The TV show Hannibal reimagined the relationship between Will Graham and Hannibal Lecter for a modern television audience.
The first lie detector machine was developed in 1921 by John Augustus Larson, a medical student and police officer.
The Hillside Stranglers, cousins Kenneth Bianchi and Angelo Buono, terrorized Los Angeles in 1977-1978.
The movie 'Murder on the Orient Express,' based on Agatha Christie's novel, has been adapted for film multiple times.
The FBI estimates that there may be 25-50 active serial killers in the United States at any given time.
The podcast 'Someone Knows Something' by CBC investigates cold cases with the cooperation of victims' families.
Touch DNA can be collected from skin cells left on surfaces, even without visible biological material.
The Whitechapel Murders file at Scotland Yard was not closed until 1892, four years after the last canonical Jack the Ripper killing.
The TV show Veronica Mars features a high school student who moonlights as a private investigator in a corrupt California town.
The Sam Sheppard murder case in 1954 inspired the TV show and movie 'The Fugitive.'
Forensic pathologists perform autopsies to determine cause and manner of death, which can be classified as natural, accident, suicide, homicide, or undetermined.
The movie 'Memento' by Christopher Nolan tells a murder mystery in reverse chronological order.
The first known use of fingerprints to solve a crime was in Buenos Aires, Argentina, in 1892, when Inspector Alvarez identified a murderer.
The Axeman of New Orleans terrorized the city in 1918-1919, and the killer was never identified.
The TV show Mare of Easttown stars Kate Winslet as a detective investigating a murder in a small Pennsylvania town.
The FBI's National Academy has trained over 50,000 law enforcement officers from all 50 states and over 170 countries.
Forensic nurses, also called Sexual Assault Nurse Examiners, are specially trained to collect evidence from victims of sexual assault.
The movie 'The Girl with the Dragon Tattoo' follows journalist Mikael Blomkvist and hacker Lisbeth Salander investigating a decades-old disappearance.
The TV show Only Murders in the Building follows three true crime podcast fans who become involved in solving a real murder in their apartment building.
Albert Fish, one of history's most disturbing serial killers, was caught when he sent a letter to one of his victim's families.
Crime mapping software helps police departments visualize patterns and predict where future crimes may occur.
Val McDermid's crime novels featuring psychologist Tony Hill have sold millions of copies and been adapted for the TV show Wire in the Blood.
The Bank of England robbery of 1836 saw thieves tunnel into the vault from a nearby sewer.
Forensic meteorology uses weather data to establish conditions at the time of a crime, such as visibility and temperature.
The movie 'Dial M for Murder' by Alfred Hitchcock centers on a husband's elaborate plan to have his wife murdered.
The TV show Broadchurch showed how media attention can both help and hinder criminal investigations.
Bruno Hauptmann was convicted and executed for the kidnapping and murder of Charles Lindbergh's infant son.
The first female FBI agents were hired in 1972, decades after the bureau's founding.
Forensic podiatry analyzes footwear impressions and gait patterns to identify suspects.
The podcast 'In the Dark' investigated the case of Curtis Flowers, who was tried six times for the same crime in Mississippi.
The movie 'Anatomy of a Murder' is considered one of the greatest courtroom dramas in film history.
The Acid Bath Murderer, John George Haigh, dissolved his victims in sulfuric acid but was caught through forensic evidence.
The TV show Mindhunter explored how serial killers like Ed Kemper, Richard Speck, and Jerry Brudos were interviewed by the FBI.
The oldest known law code, the Code of Hammurabi, prescribed specific punishments for various crimes nearly 4,000 years ago.
The trial of Oscar Pistorius for the shooting of Reeva Steenkamp was broadcast live and followed worldwide.
Forensic glass analysis can determine if glass fragments on a suspect match those from a crime scene by comparing refractive index.
Dorothy L. Sayers created the aristocratic detective Lord Peter Wimsey, one of the golden age of detective fiction's most beloved characters.
The Wichita BTK Killer's name stood for 'Bind, Torture, Kill,' which described his method of attacking victims.
The movie 'Witness for the Prosecution,' based on Agatha Christie's play, features one of cinema's most famous surprise endings.
The TV show Castle follows a mystery novelist who shadows an NYPD detective for research inspiration.
The first televised criminal trial in the United States was the 1955 trial of Harry Washburn for murder.
The Australian case of the Disappearance of the Beaumont Children in 1966 remains one of the country's most enduring mysteries.
Chain of custody documentation ensures that evidence collected at a crime scene is properly handled and admissible in court.
P.D. James created the detective Adam Dalgliesh, a Scotland Yard commander who is also a published poet.
The movie 'To Kill a Mockingbird' portrays Atticus Finch defending a wrongfully accused Black man in the 1930s South.
The TV show Monk featured Tony Shalhoub as an obsessive-compulsive former detective who solves crimes despite his phobias.
The Great Brink's Robbery of 1950 was meticulously planned over two years, with the thieves studying the building's layout and schedules.
Facial recognition technology is increasingly used by law enforcement but raises significant concerns about privacy and accuracy.
The podcast 'Bear Brook' used forensic genealogy to identify the victims found in barrels in Bear Brook State Park, New Hampshire.
The Spa Lane Murders in Sheffield, England, remain one of the UK's most baffling unsolved cases.
Forensic taphonomy studies how bodies decompose in different environments to help determine time and circumstances of death.
The movie 'Basic Instinct' features a crime novelist who becomes the prime suspect in a murder investigation.
The TV show Bosch, based on Michael Connelly's novels, streams on Amazon and follows an LAPD homicide detective.
The term 'whodunit' was first used in a 1930 book review and has since become the standard name for mystery stories.
The Elizabeth Smart kidnapping case in 2002 led to the creation of the AMBER Alert system's expansion nationwide.
Forensic scientists use UV light to detect biological fluids, fibers, and other trace evidence invisible under normal lighting.
Ross Macdonald's Lew Archer series is considered some of the finest detective fiction written in the 20th century.
The Whitey Bulger trial in 2013 revealed extensive corruption within the FBI's handling of organized crime informants.
The movie 'Se7en' was notable for its dark cinematography and having the killer turn himself in midway through the film.
The TV show Person of Interest explored themes of surveillance, privacy, and crime prevention through artificial intelligence.
Al Capone was ultimately convicted not for his violent crimes but for tax evasion, receiving an 11-year prison sentence.
Geographic information systems help forensic investigators map crime scenes in three dimensions for courtroom presentations.
The podcast 'Undisclosed' reexamined evidence from the case covered in Serial's first season.
The movie 'The Third Man' set in post-war Vienna is considered one of the greatest mystery thrillers in cinema history.
The ABC Murders by Agatha Christie features a killer who strikes in alphabetical order, taunting Poirot with letters.
The Ted Bundy case demonstrated how a charismatic appearance could allow a killer to evade suspicion for years.
Mass spectrometry is used in forensic labs to identify unknown substances found at crime scenes.
The Whitechapel Vigilance Committee was formed by local citizens in 1888 to patrol streets during the Jack the Ripper murders.
The TV show Narcos depicts the real-life rise and fall of drug cartels, blending crime with historical drama.
The trial of the Chicago Seven in 1969-1970 became one of the most politically charged courtroom battles in American history.
Forensic odontology helped identify victims of the 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami when other methods of identification were impossible.
The movie 'Heat' features one of cinema's most celebrated face-to-face scenes between Al Pacino and Robert De Niro, as a detective and a master thief.
The phrase 'beyond a reasonable doubt' is the standard of evidence required for a criminal conviction in the United States.
Israel Keyes, a serial killer active from 2001-2012, was unique in burying 'murder kits' years before committing crimes in those locations.
The TV show Line of Duty became the most-watched drama series in UK television history in 2021.
The Innocence Project was founded in 1992 by Barry Scheck and Peter Neufeld at the Benjamin N. Cardozo School of Law.
Forensic animation creates digital reconstructions of crimes to present evidence in an understandable visual format.
John le Carré's spy novels, including 'Tinker Tailor Soldier Spy,' are considered the gold standard of espionage fiction.
The Cleveland Torso Murderer dismembered at least 12 people in 1930s Cleveland, and the case was never solved.
The movie 'Insomnia' directed by Christopher Nolan follows a detective investigating a murder in an Alaskan town where the sun never sets.
The TV show Elementary reimagined Sherlock Holmes in modern-day New York with Lucy Liu as Joan Watson.
The first criminal conviction based solely on fingerprint evidence occurred in England in 1902.
The Ariel Castro kidnappings in Cleveland, where three women were held captive for over a decade, shocked the nation in 2013.
Ballistic fingerprinting compares the unique markings left on bullets and casings to link them to a specific firearm.
The podcast 'Sword and Scale' covers true crime stories with a focus on the psychological aspects of criminal behavior.
The movie 'The French Connection' is based on the real-life case of detectives who uncovered a heroin smuggling ring.
The TV show How to Get Away with Murder features a law professor and her students entangled in murder plots.
The FBI maintains a hair and fiber database that can match trace evidence to specific types of carpet, clothing, and animal breeds.
Kayo Dot's concept of 'linkage blindness' describes how police agencies fail to connect serial crimes across jurisdictions.
The Barker-Karpis gang was one of the most notorious criminal gangs during the Depression era, specializing in kidnapping and bank robbery.
Forensic podiatry was used in the UK's Cinderella case, where a shoe print led to the conviction of a murderer.
The movie 'Zodiac' was so detailed that director David Fincher required 70 takes of one diner scene.
The TV show Peaky Blinders, while focused on a crime family, explores the intersection of crime and politics in post-World War I Birmingham.
The FBI's Internet Crime Complaint Center (IC3) receives hundreds of thousands of cybercrime complaints annually.
Agatha Christie used her knowledge of pharmacy and poisons extensively in her mystery novels, accurately describing toxic effects.
The Night of the Hunter, starring Robert Mitchum as a murderous preacher, is considered one of the greatest thrillers in film history.
The TV show American Crime Story dramatized both the O.J. Simpson trial and the assassination of Gianni Versace.
Forensic palynology proved crucial in the conviction of a New Zealand man when pollen grains linked him to the burial site of his victim.
The podcast 'Last Podcast on the Left' combines humor with in-depth research on serial killers and true crime.
The movie 'Spotlight' follows Boston Globe reporters investigating systemic cover-ups, winning the Academy Award for Best Picture.
The Behavioral Evidence Analysis approach examines crime scene evidence to understand the offender's behavior and motivation.
The Amanda Knox case in Italy generated worldwide debate about wrongful conviction and media influence on criminal justice.
Advanced laser scanning can create millimeter-accurate 3D models of crime scenes for later analysis and courtroom presentation.
The TV show Criminal Minds: Beyond Borders focused on investigating crimes against Americans abroad.
The Isdal Woman case in Norway, involving an unidentified woman found dead in 1970, remains one of Northern Europe's greatest mysteries.
Forensic genealogy, the technique that caught the Golden State Killer, uses public DNA databases to identify suspects through family connections.
Rex Stout's Nero Wolfe is a famously sedentary detective who rarely leaves his brownstone, relying on assistant Archie Goodwin for legwork.
The movie 'The Departed' is a crime thriller about undercover identities within the Boston police and Irish mob, winning four Academy Awards.
The National Missing and Unidentified Persons System (NamUs) is a free database that helps resolve missing persons and unidentified remains cases.
The TV show 48 Hours on CBS takes its name from the critical first 48 hours of a murder investigation.
The Jack the Ripper letters, including the famous 'From Hell' letter, have been analyzed by handwriting experts for over a century.
Lee Child's Jack Reacher series follows a former military policeman who drifts across America encountering crime.
The Menendez brothers' trial in 1993-1996 was one of the first high-profile trials broadcast on Court TV.
Forensic bloodstain pattern analysis can reconstruct the events of a violent crime by studying the shape, size, and distribution of blood drops.
The movie 'Brick' transplants film noir conventions into a high school setting.
The TV show Dexter: New Blood revived the character eight years after the original series ended.
The Snowtown murders in South Australia involved bodies hidden in barrels of acid in an abandoned bank vault.
The first police department in the modern sense was established in London in 1829 by Sir Robert Peel.
Forensic engineering investigates structural failures and accidents to determine whether criminal negligence played a role.
The podcast 'Criminal' explores stories of people who've done wrong, been wronged, or gotten caught in the system.
The movie 'Wind River' highlights the crisis of missing and murdered Indigenous women.
The TV show White Collar follows a con artist who helps the FBI catch other white-collar criminals.
Emile Durkheim, a founding figure of sociology, argued that crime is a normal and functional part of society.
The Madeleine McCann disappearance in Portugal in 2007 became one of the most heavily reported missing person cases in history.
Alternative light sources can reveal evidence invisible to the naked eye, including biological fluids, bruises, and altered documents.
The movie 'The Bone Collector' features a quadriplegic forensics expert who directs investigations from his bed.
The TV show Hannibal was praised for its artistic visual style and complex exploration of the relationship between hunter and hunted.
The Profumo Affair in 1960s Britain combined espionage, crime, and political scandal, nearly bringing down the government.
Forensic computing can recover data from damaged or destroyed digital devices, including fire-damaged hard drives.
The podcast 'Up and Vanished' investigated the cold case of Tara Grinstead, a Georgia beauty queen who disappeared in 2005.
Tana French's Dublin Murder Squad series blends literary fiction with crime procedural storytelling.
The Son of Sam law, passed in 1977, prevents criminals from profiting off their crimes through book deals or media appearances.
The movie 'True Grit' features a young girl hiring a U.S. Marshal to track down her father's killer in the Old West.
The TV show Broadchurch inspired an American remake called Gracepoint, also starring David Tennant.
The Bow Street Runners, established in London in the 1740s, are considered one of the earliest professional police forces.
The Dahmer Netflix series in 2022 sparked renewed public interest in the psychology of serial killers and the failures of law enforcement.
Forensic soil analysis examines the mineral content, color, and composition of soil to connect evidence to specific locations.
The podcast 'Morbid' blends true crime with comedy and is one of the most downloaded crime podcasts.
The movie 'Mulholland Drive' by David Lynch is a neo-noir mystery wrapped in surrealist storytelling.
The TV show How to Get Away with Murder ran for six seasons on ABC.
The Hinterkaifeck murders in Germany in 1922, where an entire family was killed on their farm, remain unsolved.
Chain of evidence protocols require every person who handles a piece of evidence to document when and how they handled it.
The movie 'Manhunter' was the first film to feature the character of Hannibal Lecter, portrayed by Brian Cox.
The TV show Killing Eve premiered in 2018 and was praised for its fresh take on the spy thriller genre.
The Cleveland Butcher case is sometimes linked to Eliot Ness, who was Cleveland's Safety Director during the murders.
Forensic chemistry can identify accelerants used in arson cases by analyzing residue found at fire scenes.
Ian Rankin's Inspector Rebus novels capture the dark side of Edinburgh and are beloved by crime fiction readers worldwide.
The trial of Adolf Eichmann in Jerusalem in 1961 was one of the first trials to be fully televised.
The movie 'Mississippi Burning' depicted the FBI investigation into the 1964 murders of three civil rights workers.
The TV show True Crime with Aphrodite Jones was one of the first shows to focus on in-depth analysis of individual criminal cases.
Victimology, the study of victims and their relationship to the offender, is a critical component of criminal profiling.
The Night Stalker case led to one of the largest manhunts in Los Angeles history, involving over 200 officers.
The podcast 'RedHanded' covers international true crime stories often overlooked by American-focused podcasts.
Dennis Nilsen, the 'Kindly Killer,' murdered at least 12 men in London between 1978 and 1983, disposing of their remains at home.
The movie 'Zodiac' shows how the unsolved case consumed the lives of the journalists and detectives investigating it.
The TV show The Mentalist features a consultant who uses his extraordinary observation skills to help the CBI solve crimes.
The concept of 'criminal profiling' gained widespread public attention through Thomas Harris's novel 'The Silence of the Lambs.'
The Zodiac Killer's first confirmed attack occurred on December 20, 1968, when he shot two teenagers on a remote road near Vallejo, California.
Electrostatic detection apparatus (ESDA) can reveal indented writing on paper, uncovering notes written on pages placed on top.
Sara Paretsky's V.I. Warshawski was a groundbreaking female private investigator in a genre long dominated by male detectives.
The French Connection case resulted in the seizure of 112 pounds of heroin and was one of the largest narcotics busts of its era.
The TV show The Fall stars Gillian Anderson as a detective hunting a serial killer played by Jamie Dornan in Belfast.
The Enrique 'Kiki' Camarena case, in which a DEA agent was tortured and murdered in Mexico in 1985, strained U.S.-Mexico relations.
Forensic art includes not only facial reconstruction but also age progression, postmortem drawings, and composite sketches.
The movie 'Catch Me If You Can' is based on the true story of con artist Frank Abagnale Jr., who successfully impersonated multiple professionals.
CCTV footage has become one of the most important tools in modern criminal investigation, with millions of cameras worldwide.
The Toolbox Killers, Lawrence Bittaker and Roy Norris, recorded their crimes on audio tape, which was later used as evidence.
The podcast 'Anatomy of Murder' is co-hosted by a former New York City prosecutor and a journalist.
The TV show Dateline NBC has been on the air for over 30 years, making it one of the longest-running shows on network television.
Karin Slaughter's Will Trent series combines suspenseful crime fiction with complex character development.
Crime scene cleanup is a specialized profession that requires certification and knowledge of biohazard handling procedures.
The movie 'Jagged Edge' features a newspaper editor on trial for the murder of his wife, with his defense attorney becoming romantically involved.
The concept of the 'locked room mystery' was popularized by John Dickson Carr and remains a beloved subgenre of detective fiction.

Rapid DNA technology can produce a DNA profile in under two hours, compared to the days or weeks required by traditional methods.

The Great Plymouth Mail Robbery of 1962 was eclipsed by the Great Train Robbery the following year.

The movie 'Black Dahlia' dramatized the real unsolved 1947 murder case that captivated Los Angeles.
True crime conventions and events, known as CrimeCons, attract thousands of fans and industry professionals annually.
